text: They have a good sale always, so I am told. Now why is that, eh, Mademoiselle? I ask it of you as a student of human nature--why should that be?
context: Poirot asks Katherine why detective stories are popular.

text: Sometimes, Mademoiselle! Sometimes! I who speak to you--they have happened to *me*.
context: Poirot contradicts Katherine's belief that crimes happen only in fiction.

text: It is my fancy, perhaps, but I think that you have a yearning in you for interesting happenings. *Eh bien*, Mademoiselle, all through my life I have observed one thing--'All one wants one gets!' Who knows?
context: Poirot reads Katherine's appetite for adventure and reflects on desire.

text: It is true that I have the habit of being always right--but I do not boast of it. Good-night, Mademoiselle, and may you sleep well.
context: Poirot jokingly denies being a prophet before bidding Katherine good night.

text: I remember meeting you once, M. Caux, at the *Sûreté* in Paris, though doubtless you have forgotten me?
context: Poirot politely reminds Commissary Caux of an earlier meeting.

text: I came to see if I might be of any assistance?
context: Poirot offers his assistance after learning of the murder.

text: But she talked to you, did she not?
context: Poirot prompts Katherine to describe her impression of the victim.

text: Be brave, Mademoiselle; look at her well. Are you sure that this is the woman you talked to in the train yesterday?
context: Poirot steadies Katherine before she identifies the body.

text: You are an excellent witness, Mademoiselle. There is, then, no question as to the identity, but it is strange, all the same.
context: Poirot praises Katherine's careful identification while noting an anomaly.

text: If she had been struck down, it would have been comprehensible, but the man who strangled her slipped up behind and caught her unawares. A little choke--a little gurgle--that is all that would be heard, and then afterwards--that smashing blow on her face. Now why? Did he hope that if the face were unrecognizable she might not be identified? Or did he hate her so much that he could not resist striking that blow even after she was dead?
context: Poirot analyzes the strangling and the blow that disfigured the victim.

text: What is important? What is not? One cannot say at this stage. But we must note each little fact carefully.
context: Poirot explains why every small fact must be recorded early in an inquiry.

text: This shall be a *'roman policier' à nous*. We will investigate this affair together.
context: Poirot invites Katherine to investigate the case with him.

text: That is so, Monsieur. Now I enjoy the world.
context: Poirot explains his retirement to Van Aldin.

text: That is very well said, M. Van Aldin. I place myself entirely at your service.
context: Poirot accepts Van Aldin's request for help.

text: M. Van Aldin, we are all sensible, deeply sensible, of the pain it must give you to speak of these matters, but believe me, Monsieur, it is not the time for concealments. If justice is to be done, we must know everything. If you will reflect a little minute you will realize the truth of that clearly for yourself.
context: Poirot urges Van Aldin to disclose painful facts for the sake of justice.

text: There are some very famous rubies, originally part of the Crown jewels of Russia; they are unique in character, and their value is almost fabulous. There has been a rumour that they have lately passed into the possession of an American. Are we right in concluding, Monsieur, that you were the purchaser?
context: Poirot identifies the historical rubies and asks whether Van Aldin bought them.

text: There is always a pretty formidable crowd on the track of jewels such as these.
context: Poirot notes that news of famous jewels attracts professional thieves.

text: Let us arrange our facts with order and precision. According to our present theory, this is how they run. The Comte de la Roche knows of your purchase of these jewels. By an easy stratagem he induces Madame Kettering to bring the stones with her. He, then, is the man Mason saw in the train at Paris.
context: Poirot lays out the current theory in orderly form.

text: Madame is surprised to see him, but she deals with the situation promptly. Mason is got out of the way; a dinner basket is ordered. We know from the conductor that he made up the berth for the first compartment, but he did not go into the second compartment, and that a man could quite well have been concealed from him.
context: Poirot reconstructs how the supposed visitor could remain hidden on the train.

text: So far the Comte could have been hidden to a marvel. No one knows of his presence on the train except Madame; he has been careful that the maid did not see his face. All that she could say is that he was tall and dark. It is all most conveniently vague.
context: Poirot highlights the convenient vagueness in the maid's description.

text: I mean we are all agreed on one point, and that is that the Comte de la Roche knows one subject *à fond*: Women. How was it that, knowing women as he does, he did not foresee that Madame would have kept that letter?
context: Poirot questions whether a man experienced with women would overlook a saved letter.

text: *Ah, ça par exemple*, that is of no importance whatever. Naturally, if he committed the crime he will have an alibi. A man with the Comte's experience does not neglect to take precautions. No, I said *if* for a very different reason.
context: Poirot dismisses the importance of an alibi for an experienced criminal.

text: The psychology is at fault. The Comte is a scoundrel--yes. The Comte is a swindler--yes. The Comte preys upon women--yes. He proposes to steal Madame's jewels--again yes.
context: Poirot separates the Comte's known vices from the psychology of murder.

text: Is he the kind of man to commit murder? I say *no*! A man of the type of the Comte is always a coward; he takes no risks. He plays the safe, the mean, what the English call the low-down game; but murder, a hundred times no!
context: Poirot argues that the Comte's cowardly temperament is inconsistent with murder.

text: It is my business to know things, The Comte is a man of intelligence. He is at present at a villa he has leased, the Villa Marina at Antibes.
context: Poirot reveals that he already knows where the Comte is staying.

text: What will you; it is my little hobby, to know things. Naturally I have the time to indulge it. I am not overburdened with affairs.
context: Poirot modestly calls gathering information his hobby.

text: I arrange the facts, that is all.
context: Poirot says he is merely arranging the facts rather than accusing anyone.

text: Monsieur, if a doctor walks along the street and an accident happens, does he say, 'I have retired from my profession, I will continue my walk,' when there is some one bleeding to death at his feet? If I had been already in Nice, and the police had sent to me and asked me to assist them, I should have refused. But this affair, the good God thrust it upon me.
context: Poirot explains why he assists when a case is placed directly before him.

text: I accept. But in that case, I must have frank answers to my questions.
context: Poirot accepts Van Aldin as a client but demands complete frankness.

text: The dancer. Ah-ha! And Madame Kettering objected? Was she very devoted to her husband?
context: Poirot asks whether Ruth's objection arose from devotion to her husband.

text: It was not her heart that suffered, it was her pride--is that what you would say?
context: Poirot distinguishes wounded pride from wounded affection.

text: And now he has inherited two million pounds! *La vie*--it is a strange thing, is it not?
context: Poirot reflects ironically on Kettering's sudden inheritance.

text: I reflect, I speak the philosophy. But to return to where we were. Surely M. Kettering did not propose to allow himself to be divorced without making a fight for it?
context: Poirot returns from philosophizing to the practical question of resistance to divorce.

text: If you do not tell me the truth, I can do nothing.
context: Poirot refuses to proceed while Van Aldin withholds the truth.

text: I think you do. You may rest assured, M. Van Aldin, that I know how to be discreet.
context: Poirot reassures Van Aldin that he can be discreet.

text: And to-day, you are here with Hercule Poirot. How one mounts in the world!
context: Poirot jokes with George about the prestige of serving him.

text: A *morceau* of *filet de sole à la Jeannette* alighted there when I was lunching at the Ritz last Tuesday.
context: Poirot objects to a tiny stain on his waistcoat.

text: Supposing, my good Georges, that you had been born in the same social sphere as your late master, Lord Edward Frampton--that, penniless yourself, you had married an extremely wealthy wife, but that that wife proposed to divorce you, with excellent reasons, what would you do about it?
context: Poirot asks George how an aristocrat might resist a justified divorce.

text: Would he not, Georges? I wonder now. Well, perhaps you are right.
context: Poirot questions George's faith in aristocratic restraint.

text: There is a question I would like to put to Mademoiselle, if I may?
context: Poirot politely asks permission to question Ada Mason.

text: An excess of caution is suspicious, eh?
context: Poirot comments that excessive caution can itself be suspicious.

text: It is a very pretty little fortune that you inherit there, a very pretty little fortune.
context: Poirot emphasizes the fortune Derek inherits.

text: My name is Hercule Poirot, and I am probably the greatest detective in the world. You are quite sure that you did not see or speak to your wife on that train?
context: Poirot introduces himself to Derek with characteristic confidence.

text: *Then how did he know about them?*
context: Poirot asks how Derek knew about the rubies when no one had told him.

text: That is Mademoiselle Grey who speaks? *Bon.* Mademoiselle, I have a word for you from M. Van Aldin, the father of Madame Kettering. He wishes very much to speak with you, either at the Villa Marguerite or at his hotel, whichever you prefer.
context: Poirot telephones Katherine on Van Aldin's behalf.

text: This is our own Roman Policier, is it not?
context: Poirot reminds Katherine that he keeps his promises about their shared investigation.

text: I did not say so. And you, Mademoiselle, what do you think?
context: Poirot refuses to equate the official theory with his own belief.

text: Ah! Very good, you agree with me; that is just what I have said.
context: Poirot warmly approves Katherine's psychological judgment and changes the subject to Derek.

text: It is like this, you see, the Comte de la Roche has an alibi. An alibi, it is a very pestilential thing, and always open to the gravest suspicion. But here we are!
context: Poirot calls an alibi a troublesome and suspicious thing.

text: I look very English, do I not?
context: Poirot greets Katherine while displaying his English clothes.

text: You mock yourself at me, but no matter. Papa Poirot, he always laughs the last.
context: Poirot accepts Katherine's teasing and boasts that he laughs last.

text: You are wrong, in these matters one needs not energy but finesse. At the tennis one meets every one. That is so important. Ah, there is Mr. Kettering.
context: Poirot contrasts energy with finesse and explains why social observation matters.

text: It is amazing, M. Kettering, how well you speak the French, so well that you could be taken for a Frenchman if you chose. That is a very rare accomplishment among Englishmen.
context: Poirot remarks on Derek's unusually fluent French.

text: It was Mademoiselle here who mentioned having seen you going in.
context: Poirot apologetically reveals that Katherine saw Derek enter the compartment.

text: Do not enrage yourself, I beg of you. Every one has his own methods. Me, I have always found it a delightful and pleasing idea to combine business and pleasure together.
context: Poirot defends combining business with pleasure.

text: As you say--a Greek. He is a dealer in antiques of world-wide reputation. He has a small shop in Paris, and he is suspected by the police of being something more.
context: Poirot identifies Papopolous as an antique dealer suspected of receiving stolen goods.

text: I ask myself, I, Hercule Poirot ask myself *why is M. Papopolous suddenly come to Nice?*
context: Poirot dramatically asks why Papopolous has suddenly come to Nice.

text: By the roadside, no. *Ça n'est pas pratique.* But listen now--me, I have made a little suggestion to M. Carrège. He is graciously pleased to approve of it. In each Bureau de Poste in the neighbourhood it has been seen to that there is some one who knows the Comte de la Roche by sight. Because, you see, Monsieur, the best way of hiding a thing is by sending it away by the post.
context: Poirot explains why posting an object is safer than hiding it nearby.

text: Order, method, being prepared for eventualities beforehand--that is all there is to it.
context: Poirot attributes his success to order, method, and preparation.

text: Grant me a little moment, Monsieur. Me, I have a little idea. Many people have mocked themselves at the little ideas of Hercule Poirot--and they have been wrong.
context: Poirot asks for time and defends the value of his little ideas.

text: You are shrewd, M. Papopolous. No, it is not. They are not, for instance, worth five hundred thousand dollars.
context: Poirot tells Papopolous the displayed rubies are not worth the supposed fortune.

text: *I am not concerned with the recovery of these jewels. That is the affair of the police.* I am working not for the police but for M. Van Aldin. I want to lay hands on the man who killed Madame Kettering. I am interested in the jewels only in so far as they may lead me to the man.
context: Poirot states that he seeks the murderer rather than the stolen jewels.

text: But to continue. When a Russian Grand Duke, an Austrian Archduchess, or an Italian Prince wish to dispose of their family jewels--to whom do they go? To M. Papopolous, is it not? He who is famous all over the world for the discretion with which he arranges these things.
context: Poirot flatters Papopolous while pressing him for help.

text: I think there is. You may remember, M. Papopolous, that seventeen years ago there was a certain article in your hands, left there as security by a very--er--Prominent Person. It was in your keeping and it unaccountably disappeared. You were, if I may use the English expression, in the soup.
context: Poirot invokes an old debt to persuade Papopolous to share information.

text: Seventeen years is a long time, but I believe that I am right in saying, Monsieur, that your race does not forget.
context: Poirot appeals to Papopolous's long memory and sense of obligation.

text: You and your father will perhaps dine with me one night.
context: Poirot gallantly assures Zia that age has not changed her and invites her to dinner.

text: I am always punctual, The exactitude--always do I observe it.
context: Poirot praises punctuality, order, and method.

text: I have come to ask you yet more questions, We must get to the bottom of this affair. Always I return to the question of the man in the train. You have been shown the Comte de la Roche. You say that it is possible he was the man, but you are not sure.
context: Poirot returns methodically to the uncertain identification of the man on the train.

text: Just so, Mademoiselle, but reflect a minute. The train has just arrived at the Gare de Lyon. Many of the passengers promenade themselves upon the quay. Your mistress was about to do so, and for that purpose had doubtless put on her fur coat, eh?
context: Poirot reconstructs why Derek might have been wearing an overcoat and hat at the station.

text: Your master, then, does the same. The train is heated, but outside in the station it is cold. He puts on his overcoat and his hat and he walks along beside the train, and looking up at the lighted windows he suddenly sees Madame Kettering. Until then he has had no idea that she was on the train. Naturally, he mounts the carriage and goes to her compartment.
context: Poirot completes a plausible reconstruction of Derek discovering his wife on the train.

text: You ask me a question; I answer it. You say have I not shown you the jewels yesterday? I reply--no. What I showed you, M. Van Aldin, was a first-class imitation, hardly to be distinguished except by an expert from the real ones.
context: Poirot explains that the jewels he displayed were only an expert imitation.

text: Certainly. There is no doubt as to that. See, I will recount to you the affair as I see it. The Comte knew of the rubies and he laid his plans accordingly.
context: Poirot begins reconstructing the Comte's intended jewel substitution.

text: He made up a romantic story of a book he was writing, so as to induce your daughter to bring them with her. He provided himself with an exact duplicate. It is clear, is it not, that substitution is what he was after.
context: Poirot explains the Comte's planned substitution of imitation rubies.

text: Then you happened to mention that you had once mistaken the Comte for your son-in-law. That told me that they were of the same height and build, and alike in colouring. It put some curious ideas in my head. The maid had only been with your daughter a short time. It was unlikely that she would know Mr. Kettering well by sight, since he had not been living in Curzon Street; also the man was careful to keep his face turned away.
context: Poirot explains why Derek's resemblance to the Comte and the maid's unfamiliarity mattered.

text: No, no, I did not say that--but it is a possibility--a very strong possibility. He was in a tight corner, a very tight corner, threatened with ruin. This was the one way out.
context: Poirot carefully distinguishes suspicion from a proven accusation.

text: Leave it in the hands of Hercule Poirot, have no fears. I will discover the truth.
context: Poirot grandly assures Van Aldin that he will discover the truth.

text: You will pardon an old man, Monsieur, if he says to you something that you may consider impertinent. There is one of your English proverbs that I would quote to you. It says that 'it is well to be off with the old love, before being on with the new.'
context: Poirot warns Derek with an English proverb about ending one romance before beginning another.

text: I would not say that. Your reputation is bad, yes, but with women--never does that deter them. If you were a man of excellent character, of strict morality who had done nothing that he should not do, and--possibly everything that he should do--*eh bien!* then I should have grave doubts of your success. Moral worth, you understand, it is not romantic. It is appreciated, however, by widows.
context: Poirot argues that moral perfection is less romantic to women than a bad reputation.

text: The personality of a criminal, Georges, is an interesting matter. Many murderers are men of great personal charm.
context: Poirot reflects that many murderers possess great personal charm.

text: I shall go to bed and take a *tisane*. The expected has happened, and when the expected happens, it always causes me emotion.
context: Poirot reacts to an expected development by retiring with a tisane.

text: And so it is, that we are the good friends and have no secrets from each other.
context: Poirot asserts friendship and mutual confidence with Katherine.

text: *Ah, mais c'est Anglais ça*, everything in black and white, everything clear cut and well defined. But life, it is not like that, Mademoiselle. There are the things that are not yet, but which cast their shadow before.
context: Poirot contrasts English clarity with life's ambiguous shadows.

text: If you had said in an indifferent voice, 'Oh, quite nice,' *eh bien*, do you know I should have been better pleased.
context: Poirot reads emotional significance into Katherine's enthusiastic answer about Knighton.

text: You do not see why I am being so impertinent, Mademoiselle? I am an old man, and now and then--not very often--I come across some one whose welfare is dear to me. We are friends, Mademoiselle. You have said so yourself. And it is just this--I should like to see you happy.
context: Poirot explains that friendship makes Katherine's happiness important to him.

text: Perhaps you are right, Mademoiselle. See you, I who speak to you have seen much of the world, and I know that there are two things which are true. A good man may be ruined by his love for a bad woman--but the other way holds good also. A bad man may equally be ruined by his love for a good woman.
context: Poirot reflects that love can ruin good and bad people alike.

text: There are others--more dangerous than the Comte de la Roche. They have qualities that appeal--recklessness, daring, audacity. You are fascinated, Mademoiselle; I see that, but I think that it is no more than that. I hope so.
context: Poirot warns Katherine about the fascination of reckless and audacious men.

text: You could, perhaps, love a thief, Mademoiselle, *but not a murderer*.
context: Poirot draws a moral boundary between loving a thief and loving a murderer.

text: M. Van Aldin, if I may say so, behaved exceedingly foolishly.
context: Poirot agrees that Van Aldin was foolish to refuse Mirelle an interview.

text: M. Van Aldin is an obstinate man, I do not argue with obstinate men. I act in spite of them. We will go and see the lady immediately. I will tell her that you are empowered by M. Van Aldin to act for him, and you will guard yourself well from contradicting me.
context: Poirot says he acts around obstinate men rather than arguing with them.

text: He is indisposed, the Riviera throat, it has him in its grip, but me, I am empowered to act for him, as is Major Knighton, his secretary. Unless, of course, Mademoiselle would prefer to wait a fortnight or so.
context: Poirot invents a polite illness to make Mirelle speak immediately.

text: It is necessary to act while--how do you say it?--the iron is hot, She is temperamental; in an hour's time, maybe, she will repent, and she will wish to draw back. We must prevent that at all costs.
context: Poirot explains why a temperamental witness must be taken to the magistrate at once.

text: I was not aware that the fact had been mentioned outside the Examining Magistrate's office.
context: Poirot catches Mirelle referring to confidential knowledge.

text: On the contrary, I think it agrees with them very well. Au revoir, Messieurs; if news of any importance comes to me I will communicate it to you immediately.
context: Poirot says new information about the Marquis fits his private ideas.

text: I have been the cat, watching at the mouse hole. I have been the good dog following up the scent, and not taking my nose from the trail. And also, my good Georges, I have been the squirrel. I have stored away the little fact here, the little fact there. I go now to my store and I take out one particular nut, a nut that I stored away--let me see, seventeen years ago.
context: Poirot compares his method of storing facts to a squirrel collecting nuts.

text: See, I admit it frankly. As a rule I know everything, but in this case, I--well, I do not. You, perhaps, know better than I do.
context: Poirot candidly admits uncertainty about Katherine's feelings.

text: Mademoiselle, what you have said there is very just--very true. I was struggling in darkness, and you have shown me a light. There was a point that puzzled me and you have made it plain.
context: Poirot tells Lenox that her idea has illuminated a point that puzzled him.

text: But I will tell you this, Mademoiselle. I am not satisfied; no, I, Hercule Poirot, am not yet satisfied. It may be that this very night I shall learn something more. At least, I go to try.
context: Poirot declares that he remains unsatisfied despite Derek's arrest.

text: Some one who might know something. In these matters one must leave no stone unturned. Au revoir, Mademoiselle.
context: Poirot says every possible source of information must be pursued.

text: You are the daughter of your father, Mademoiselle Zia. To know when to stop. Ah! that is the art.
context: Poirot praises Zia for knowing when to stop gambling.

text: So it is said. Men are foolish, are they not, Mademoiselle? To eat, to drink, to breathe the good air, it is a very pleasant thing, Mademoiselle. One is foolish to leave all that simply because one has no money--or because the heart aches. *L'amour*, it causes many fatalities, does it not?
context: Poirot reflects that life is worth keeping despite poverty or heartbreak.

text: When I look at you, it seems much less, You were then very much as you are now, Mademoiselle, a little thinner, a little paler, a little more serious. Sixteen years old and fresh from your pension. Not quite the *petite pensionnaire*, not quite a woman. You were very delicious, very charming, Mademoiselle Zia; others thought so too, without doubt.
context: Poirot gallantly recalls Zia as a sixteen-year-old girl.

text: That may be, yes, that well may be. At sixteen one is credulous, is one not? One believes what one is told.
context: Poirot observes that youth is credulous.

text: I guessed. It was a good guess, was it not, Mademoiselle? You see, unless you are good at guessing, it is not much use being a detective.
context: Poirot explains that his detective success came from a good guess.

text: But no, but no! Debarrass yourself of that idea, Mademoiselle. I am not a blackmailer. I do not hold your secret over your head and threaten you with it.
context: Poirot assures Zia that he will not blackmail her with an old secret.

text: Listen, and I will tell you why. Women, Mademoiselle, are generous. If they can render a service to one who has rendered a service to them, they will do it. I was generous once to you, Mademoiselle. When I might have spoken, I held my tongue.
context: Poirot appeals to generosity and reciprocity rather than coercion.

text: You are young, Mademoiselle, but there are three things that cannot be hurried--*le bon Dieu*, Nature, and old people.
context: Poirot says that God, nature, and old people cannot be hurried.

text: *Ma foi*, I needed it! I am now a lion--a giant. Ah, Mademoiselle Katherine, you have not seen me as I can be. You have seen the gentle, the calm Hercule Poirot; but there is another Hercule Poirot. I go now to bully, to threaten, to strike terror into the hearts of those who listen to me.
context: Poirot theatrically contrasts his gentle manner with his intimidating investigative side.

text: Permit me to say that you have been badly educated. It is the name of one of the great ones of this world.
context: Poirot rebukes the servants for not recognizing his famous name.

text: Ah, yes; on seven occasions at least. I will recite them to you.
context: Poirot calmly lists the servants' previous lies.

text: But it is not of these past lapses that I wish to speak, only, my dear friend, do not get into the habit of thinking yourself too clever. I come now to the particular lie in which I am concerned--your statement that the Comte de la Roche arrived at this villa on the morning of 14th January.
context: Poirot warns a servant not to think himself too clever before naming the relevant lie.

text: Odd, how one remembers some things--and forgets others.
context: Poirot points out the selective nature of memory.

text: Yes, yes, it is as I say. You tell your lies and you think nobody knows. But there are two people who know. Yes--two people. One is *le bon Dieu*--
context: Poirot says both God and another observer know when people lie.

text: And the other is Hercule Poirot.
context: Poirot names himself as the other witness to their lies.

text: Am I to stop here all day, arguing with a couple of imbeciles thus? It is the truth I want. If you will not give it to me, that is your look out. *For the last time, when did Monsieur le Comte arrive at the Villa Marina--Tuesday morning or Wednesday morning?*
context: Poirot angrily demands the truth about the Comte's arrival date.

text: I tell you I know what happened as well as though I had been there. You went into her compartment and you found her dead. I tell you I know it. To lie to me is dangerous. Be careful, Mademoiselle Mirelle.
context: Poirot warns Mirelle that lying to him is dangerous because he knows what happened.

text: I said to myself, 'If you want to know anything about the dramatic profession there is one person who knows all that is to be known and that is my old friend, Mr. Joseph Aarons.'
context: Poirot flatters theatrical agent Joseph Aarons before seeking information.

text: *Précisément.* Now I want to ask you, Monsieur Aarons, what you know about a young woman called Kidd.
context: Poirot precisely names Kitty Kidd as the woman he is tracing.

text: A ruby the size of a pigeon's egg!
context: Poirot reacts keenly to news of Mirelle's enormous ruby.

text: But if I remember rightly, the ruby that is named 'Heart of Fire' is the centre stone in a necklace.
context: Poirot recalls that the Heart of Fire belongs at the center of a necklace.

text: No, no--somehow I do not think it is coloured glass.
context: Poirot quietly rejects the idea that Mirelle's stone is merely glass.

text: Yes, you are older. And by that I do not mean that the wrinkles and the crows' feet are coming. When I first saw you, Mademoiselle, you were a looker-on at life. You had the quiet, amused look of one who sits back in the stalls and watches the play.
context: Poirot tells Katherine that she has changed from an observer into a participant.

text: Now, you no longer watch. It is an absurd thing, perhaps, that I say here, but you have the wary look of a fighter who is playing a difficult game.
context: Poirot describes Katherine as a wary fighter in a difficult game.

text: You have heard me speak of my friend Hastings?--he who said that I was a human oyster. *Eh bien*, Mademoiselle, I have met my match in you. You, far more than I, play a lone hand.
context: Poirot compares Katherine's reserve with Hastings's description of him as a human oyster.

text: Never does Hercule Poirot talk nonsense. It is as I say.
context: Poirot insists that he never talks nonsense.

text: I see that that tells you nothing. But I will not be a human oyster. No, I will lay my cards on the table, which is assuredly a thing that oysters do not do. You suspect, do you not, that I am not satisfied with the case against Derek Kettering?
context: Poirot says he will lay his cards on the table instead of behaving like an oyster.

text: *Eh bien*, Mademoiselle, what I have done I do not regret. I have only one duty--to discover the truth, and that way led straight to Mr. Kettering. But did it end there? The police say yes, but I, Hercule Poirot, am not satisfied.
context: Poirot explains that truth, not the police's conclusion, is his only duty.

text: I have asked myself, Mademoiselle, a hundred times, 'Was Derek Kettering the kind of man who would deal that smashing blow after having committed the murder?' What end would it serve? What purpose would it accomplish? Was it a likely action for one of Monsieur Kettering's temperament? And, Mademoiselle, the answer to these questions is profoundly unsatisfactory.
context: Poirot repeatedly questions the purpose and psychology of the blow to the victim's face.

text: Yes, as you say, from the Ritz. I am a luxurious person sometimes--when a millionaire pays.
context: Poirot jokes about luxury when a millionaire pays his expenses.

text: No, with the Press--a much more deadly weapon.
context: Poirot calls the press a deadlier threat than the police.

text: A mirror shows the truth, but every one stands in a different place for looking into the mirror.
context: Poirot compares different viewpoints to people looking into the same mirror.

text: Courage, Mademoiselle, you must not fail now; the end is very near.
context: Poirot urges Katherine to keep courage because the end is near.

text: No, no, *pas la guerre*--a memory of my days in the police force, a bullet of a rascally Apache.
context: Poirot dismisses concern about an old bullet wound with a French phrase.

text: I am silent, but all the same I say it is a pity. And now, Messieurs, let us come to business.
context: Poirot ends a personal digression and turns the men back to business.

text: I am not mad. I am eccentric, perhaps--at least certain people say so; but as regards my profession, I am very much, as one says, 'all there.' I ask you, Monsieur Van Aldin, whether you would be glad or sorry if what I tell you should be the case?
context: Poirot distinguishes eccentricity from professional madness and asks Van Aldin to face his feelings.

text: I have my own methods. The exercise of a little tact, a little cleverness--and the thing is done.
context: Poirot attributes his success in breaking an alibi to tact and cleverness.

text: It is as yet only a theory, but I tell you this, Monsieur Van Aldin, the facts are worth investigating. You must come out with me to the south of France and go into the case on the spot.
context: Poirot calls his new idea a theory worth testing on the scene.

text: *Mon Dieu*, but that is something I have not thought of! We must break our journey in Paris. Quick, quick, let us alight at once.
context: Poirot suddenly insists on breaking the journey in Paris.

text: *Ma foi*, I lose my head nowadays. Let us return and continue our journey quietly. With reasonable luck the train will not have gone.
context: Poirot admits that he has behaved like an imbecile and calmly reverses course.

text: I am like the cat upon the hot tiles. Every little noise it makes me jump.
context: Poirot pretends to be nervous and jumpy during the train journey.

text: You may not remember, Monsieur Van Aldin, but Miss Grey in her evidence mentioned that a youth in a cap and overcoat descended on to the platform ostensibly to stretch his legs. Me, I think that that youth was most probably a woman.
context: Poirot reasons that the youth seen leaving at Lyons was probably a woman.

text: Her name--or the name under which she was known, for many years--is Kitty Kidd, but you, Monsieur Van Aldin, knew her by another name--*that of Ada Mason*.
context: Poirot identifies the disguised woman as Kitty Kidd, known to Van Aldin as Ada Mason.

text: Permit me to offer you a cigarette--out of your own cigarette case. It was careless of you to drop it when you boarded the train on the *ceinture* at Paris.
context: Poirot returns Knighton's cigarette case and reveals a key piece of evidence.

text: No, don't move, the door into the next compartment is open, and you are being covered from there this minute. I unbolted the door into the corridor when we left Paris, and our friends the police were told to take their places there. As I expect you know, the French police want you rather urgently, Major Knighton--or shall we say--Monsieur le Marquis?
context: Poirot reveals the police ambush and identifies Knighton as the Marquis.

text: Yes, I will give you explanations. It began with the one point that puzzled me. You know what that point was? *The disfigured face.*
context: Poirot begins his final explanation with the clue that first troubled him.

text: It is not an uncommon thing to find when investigating a crime and it rouses an immediate question, the question of identity. That naturally was the first thing that occurred to me. Was the dead woman really Mrs. Kettering?
context: Poirot explains why a disfigured face raises the question of identity.

text: But I am a good detective. I suspect. There is nobody and nothing that I do not suspect. I believe nothing that I am told.
context: Poirot states his fundamental habit of universal suspicion.

text: I say to myself: how do we know that Ada Mason was left behind in Paris? And at first the answer to that question seemed completely satisfactory.
context: Poirot reopens a fact everyone else had accepted.

text: I concentrated on the chief stumbling-block to my theory, Major Knighton's statement that he saw Ada Mason at the Ritz after the Blue Train had left Paris. That seemed conclusive enough, but yet, on examining the facts carefully, I noted two things. First, that by a curious coincidence he, too, had been exactly two months in your service. Secondly, his initial letter was the same--'K.'
context: Poirot identifies two small coincidences that weakened Knighton's evidence.

text: Supposing--just supposing--that it was *his* cigarette case which had been found in the carriage. Then, if Ada Mason and he were working together, and she recognized it when we showed it to her, would she not act precisely as she had done?
context: Poirot tests the hypothesis that the cigarette case belonged to Knighton.

text: That is the simplicity of the whole thing. *There was no man.* Ah--*mille tonnerres!*--do you not see the cleverness of it all? Whose word have we for it that there ever was a man there? Only Ada Mason's. And we believe in Ada Mason because of Knighton's evidence that she was left behind in Paris.
context: Poirot reveals that the supposed man seen in Paris never existed.

text: Ah! I am coming to that. We have Mrs. Kettering's own evidence there, but, on the other hand, we have not really got her evidence, because, Monsieur Van Aldin, a dead woman cannot give evidence. It is not *her* evidence, but the evidence of the conductor of the train--a very different affair altogether.
context: Poirot distinguishes a dead woman's evidence from a conductor's report of it.

text: No, no, not at all. He spoke what he thought to be the truth. But the woman who told him that she had left her maid in Paris was not Mrs. Kettering.
context: Poirot says the conductor honestly mistook another woman for Ruth.

text: Monsieur Van Aldin, Ruth Kettering was dead before the train arrived at the Gare de Lyon. It was Ada Mason, dressed in her mistress's very distinctive clothing, who purchased a dinner basket and who made that very necessary statement to the conductor.
context: Poirot reveals that Ruth was already dead before the train reached Paris.

text: No, no, Monsieur Van Aldin; not impossible. *Les femmes*, they look so much alike nowadays that one identifies them more by their clothing than by their faces. Ada Mason was the same height as your daughter.
context: Poirot explains how clothing and physical similarity enabled the impersonation.

text: Dressed in that very sumptuous fur coat and the little red lacquer hat jammed down over her eyes, with just a bunch of auburn curls showing over each ear, it was no wonder that the conductor was deceived. He had not previously spoken to Mrs. Kettering, you remember.
context: Poirot details the disguise that deceived the conductor.

text: First, bear it in mind that the crime was planned and undertaken by the two of them--Knighton and Ada Mason, working together. Knighton was in Paris that day on your business. He boarded the train somewhere on its way round the *ceinture*.
context: Poirot identifies the two conspirators and places Knighton on the train.

text: Perhaps he draws her attention to something out the window, and as she turns to look he slips the cord round her neck--and the whole thing is over in a second or two. The door of the compartment is locked, and he and Ada Mason set to work.
context: Poirot reconstructs the murder and the immediate staging of the crime.

text: At the Gare de Lyon Ada Mason gets a dinner basket, and shutting herself into the toilet compartment she quickly changes into her mistress's clothes, adjusts two false bunches of auburn curls, and generally makes up to resemble her as closely as possible. When the conductor comes to make up the bed, she tells him the prepared story about having left her maid behind in Paris; and whilst he is making up the berth, she stands looking out of the window, so that her back is towards the corridor and people passing along there.
context: Poirot reconstructs Ada Mason's impersonation at the Gare de Lyon.

text: As soon as the conductor had swung himself down on to the platform at Lyons, she follows, slouching along as though just taking a breath of air. At a moment when she is unobserved, she hurriedly crosses to the other platform, and takes the first train back to Paris and the Ritz Hotel.
context: Poirot explains how Ada Mason left the train at Lyons.

text: One of the chief assets of the gentleman called the Marquis was his plausible, ingratiating manner. You fell a victim to his charm, Monsieur Van Aldin, when you engaged him as a secretary on such a slight acquaintanceship.
context: Poirot explains why Knighton's charm made his deception effective.

text: It was very astutely done--so astutely done that it deceived a man whose knowledge of other men is as great as yours is.
context: Poirot praises the subtlety of the scheme that deceived Van Aldin.

text: Yes, yes; that was part of the game. As Richard Knighton his life was quite free from reproach. He was well born, well connected, did honourable service in the War, and seemed altogether above suspicion; but when I came to glean information about the mysterious Marquis, I found many points of similarity.
context: Poirot explains how Knighton's respectable identity shielded the Marquis.

text: This is not the first murder that lies to the Marquis's charge. He is a killer by instinct; he believes, too, in leaving no evidence behind him. Dead men and women tell no tales.
context: Poirot characterizes the Marquis as an instinctive killer.

text: But like all great men--and the Marquis was a great man--he had his weaknesses. He fell genuinely in love with Miss Grey, and suspecting her liking for Derek Kettering, he could not resist the temptation to saddle him with the crime when the opportunity presented itself.
context: Poirot identifies jealousy as the Marquis's decisive weakness.

text: That suggested camouflage. When I was in London I went to the surgeon in question, and I got several technical details from him which confirmed me in that belief. I mentioned the name of that surgeon in Knighton's hearing the day before yesterday. The natural thing would have been for Knighton to mention that he had been attended by him during the War, but he said nothing--and that little point, if nothing else, gave me the last final assurance that my theory of the crime was correct.
context: Poirot explains how Knighton's false limp became the final confirmation of his theory.

text: I am only Hercule Poirot, yet, as you say, in my own way I am a big man, even as you also are a big man. I am glad and happy to have been of service to you. Now I go to repair the damages caused by travel. Alas! my excellent Georges is not with me.
context: Poirot modestly compares his stature with Van Aldin's and takes leave.

text: Yes, she might have trusted you. But Mademoiselle Katherine has spent a great deal of her life listening, and those who have listened do not find it easy to talk; they keep their sorrows and joys to themselves and tell no one.
context: Poirot explains Katherine's reserve to Lenox.

text: Yes, Mademoiselle. It was you who gave me the first inkling of the truth when you said that the person who committed the crime need not have been on the train at all. Before that, I could not see how the thing had been done.
context: Poirot credits Lenox with the insight that first opened the solution.

text: Yes, yes, I know. Life is like a train, Mademoiselle. It goes on. And it is a good thing that that is so.
context: Poirot compares life to a train that continues onward.

text: Yes--yes, it is true. You are young, younger than you yourself know. Trust the train, Mademoiselle, for it is *le bon Dieu* who drives it.
context: Poirot urges Lenox to trust life's journey because God drives it.

text: And trust Hercule Poirot. He knows.
context: Poirot closes by asking Lenox to trust both the train and him.
